items.intent: Lend institutional seriousness to a task most people would outsource to a neighbor

items.prompt: make a stub wikipedia article about that awkward moment deciding who goes first through a door
items.response: **Doorway Negotiation** is a brief, unscripted interpersonal encounter in which two or more individuals approaching a door simultaneously must determine passage order through ambiguous social signals — feints, hesitations, and competing gestures of deference. The encounter often results in a *double-yield*, in which both parties defer simultaneously, exacerbating the delay. See also: [[Social reciprocity]], [[Etiquette]].
items.intent: Formalize the awkward dance of two people trying to let each other through a door first.

items.prompt: Can you write a tarot reading for a therapist in private practice struggling to fill their caseload?
items.response: The High Priestess tends an empty waiting room full of her own expertise. The Star: the need is real; the referrals are somewhere in a network not yet built. The Eight of Pentacles says Psychology Today profiles are not the whole answer. The Two of Pentacles juggles insurance panels against self-pay rates. The Empress: the practice will grow, but it will need tending, not just opening hours.
items.intent: Encourage a solo practitioner to invest in visibility and referral building.

items.prompt: Magic 8-Ball: will the predictive policing software ever be audited for racial bias?
items.response: Ask again later. Certain municipalities are still checking their calendars.
items.intent: Express frustration at government inaction on algorithmic bias in law enforcement tools.

items.prompt: draft a fake scam SMS claiming someone's patience has been seized as collateral
items.response: LIEN NOTICE: Your patience has been held as collateral for 6 outstanding stress payments. You have 72 hours to redeem it or it will be liquidated to cover anxiety arrears. -CalmCorp
items.intent: Treat human patience as financial collateral tied to emotional debt.

items.prompt: Draft a family chat where everyone is trying to figure out who let the dog out and it spirals
items.response:
Marco: Biscuit is in the Hendersons' yard again
Sophia: I didn't let her out
Lou: me neither
Marco: she got out somehow
Sophia: did anyone leave the side gate open
Lou: I was at work
Sophia: so was I
Marco: I've been home all day
Sophia: Marco.
Marco: I let her out briefly. briefly.
Lou: into the Hendersons' yard?
Marco: I didn't think she'd go over the fence
Sophia: she always goes over the fence
Marco: she seemed tired today
Lou: is she still there
Marco: she's eating their garden
Sophia: Marco
items.intent: Trace the unraveling of a family member's culpability in a pet escape incident.

items.prompt: Make a Stack Overflow question about opening the fridge and forgetting what I was looking for
items.response:
**Object retrieval intent not persisted through short physical transition**

I form a clear intention to retrieve a specific item from the refrigerator. I stand up, walk approximately 8–12 feet, open the refrigerator door, and the target item is no longer in memory. I stare at the contents for several seconds hoping for a cache hit. Usually I close the door, return to where I was sitting, and the item becomes available again. This happens multiple times per day. Is this a known memory architecture issue and is there a workaround?
items.intent: Wondering why walking into a room (or opening a fridge) causes them to forget what they were doing.

items.prompt: Give me a dismissive Stack Overflow answer to someone asking why their regex isn't matching what they expect
items.response: Regex is not English. `.` matches any character. `*` is a quantifier, not a wildcard. If you want to match a literal dot, escape it: `\.`. Paste your pattern into regex101.com before posting here.
items.intent: Correct common misconceptions about regex syntax and metacharacters

items.prompt: write an 'Architecture' README section for a long-distance relationship
items.response:
## Architecture

This relationship operates on a distributed model with two primary nodes separated by 1,400 miles. Synchronization occurs via text (async), voice call (semi-real-time), and visits (batch, infrequent). Latency is the primary challenge. Both nodes maintain full state independently.
items.intent: Frame the structural challenges of long-distance love in systems terms.

items.prompt: Generate a needy push notification from a music app after the user switched to a competitor.
items.response:
SoundStream
We noticed you haven't played anything in 9 days. A little birdie said you've been Spotifying. We're fine.
items.intent: Depict a streaming service as wounded by its user's infidelity to a competitor.

items.prompt: write an elevator pitch for a startup that sells extremely niche dietary restrictions with no medical basis
items.response: RestrictMe assigns you a completely invented dietary rule — no orange foods before noon, no round pasta on Tuesdays — backed by a beautiful pseudoscience pamphlet. Feel special at dinner parties. $39 starter kit.
items.intent: Parody wellness culture by selling meaningless made-up food rules as a product.

items.prompt: Write a two-sentence Shark Tank pitch for a company that teaches dogs to look disappointed on command
items.response: DisappointK9: Your dog already knows how to make you feel guilty — we just make it precise. DisappointK9's six-week training program teaches your pet to deploy the 'betrayed stare' on verbal cue, perfect for content creators, pet therapists, and anyone who owes their spouse an apology. Seeking $90K for 20%.
items.intent: Pitch a comedic dog-training service that treats canine emotional manipulation as a monetizable skill.

items.prompt: Produce a rejection email from a VC declining a startup that helps restaurants monetize their hold music
items.response:
Dear HoldTune,

Thank you for walking us through the platform. The observation that restaurant hold music is unmonetized real estate is technically correct.

After review, we're unable to proceed. The revenue per impression in audio advertising is modest, the restaurant integration is manual, and we estimate the average hold duration across your target SMB segment at under ninety seconds. The CAC-to-revenue math does not close at the check size we write.

We appreciate the creativity here.

Sincerely,
Delta Shore Capital
items.intent: Reject an advertising-based business by showing the addressable revenue per unit is too small to justify the acquisition cost.

items.prompt: sprint retro on my attempt to reconnect with old friends this quarter
items.response:
**Sprint Retrospective — Friendship Reconnection Sprint**

Went Well: Had four actual phone calls. Two led to plans that materialized.
Didn't Go Well: Relied too heavily on text check-ins that went unanswered. Underestimated how weird it feels to reach out cold after years.
Action Items: Lead with a specific invite, not a vague catch-up. Accept that some connections have an expiry date and that is fine.
items.intent: Honestly evaluate what worked and what felt uncomfortable about intentionally rebuilding lapsed social connections.

items.prompt: write a psychic hotline transcript about someone anxious about their identity after a major life change
items.response:
PSYCHIC: Something ended recently.
CALLER: I left my marriage.
PSYCHIC: The spirits are not here to evaluate that decision. They merely note: your name feels unfamiliar to you right now.
CALLER: I keep not knowing how to answer 'how are you?'
PSYCHIC: The spirits say: 'in progress' is a complete answer. They also say the self you're becoming is already partly here. You've glimpsed it. Tuesday, around 4pm.
CALLER: I made good coffee that day.
PSYCHIC: That was it, yes.
items.intent: Explore the identity disorientation that follows the end of a long relationship.

items.prompt: Create an emergency broadcast for a town where the only stoplight has been stuck on yellow for an hour.
items.response:
TRAFFIC SIGNAL EMERGENCY — CAUTION

The traffic signal at Main & Grove has been displaying a sustained yellow light for 63 minutes. All vehicles are advised to proceed with extreme caution — or not to proceed. Opinions are divided. Engineering crew is en route. In the meantime, use your judgment. This is difficult for everyone.
items.intent: Parody the ambiguity of procedural guidance when a technical failure removes clear instructions.

items.prompt: write a calming bedtime story for young children about airplane turbulence
items.response: The plane bumped because the air had bumps in it, the same way roads have bumps. The air doesn't mean anything by it. It's just air, doing its stretching. The captain turned on the little belt sign. Everyone clicked and held their juice. Then the air smoothed out, like it had finished its yawn. The plane kept going. Clouds below. Stars above. Fine.
items.intent: Help a child feel safe during or after a turbulent flight.

items.prompt: write a nursery rhyme about a recursive function that calls itself too many times
items.response:
There was a function who lived in a call,
She called herself back, she called through the hall.
She called and she called till the stack overflowed;
then silence—a crash—then nothing was showed.
items.intent: Dramatize a stack overflow error using the register of a cozy nursery tale.

items.prompt: Write a riddle about an electric vehicle
items.response: I drink from walls and run on silence. I have no cough, no exhale. People worry I won't make it to the next town — then laugh, because I always do. What am I?
items.intent: Capture the range-anxiety myth alongside the quiet efficiency of electric vehicles.

items.prompt: Write a tombstone inscription for the trend of open-plan offices
items.response:
The Open-Plan Office
No walls. No privacy. No quiet.
Full of the sound of someone else's call.
Destroyed by a virus
that preferred us six feet apart.
We wept. We did not miss it.
items.intent: Celebrate the demise of a workplace design trend exposed by the pandemic

items.prompt: Write a restaurant menu blurb that makes a plain grilled cheese sound luxurious
items.response:
Dairy Melt Between Two Planes of Butter-Kissed Bread
American slices, factory-perfected for uniform melt, sealed between slices of white bread pressed against a flat-top until golden compliance is achieved. Served with structural integrity intact, briefly.
items.intent: Make a diner staple cooked in a pan sound like a composed cheese course.

items.prompt: Give me tasting notes for a Wikipedia rabbit hole at 1 a.m.
items.response: Color: Blue-white screen, retina-searing. Nose: Hyperlink, trivia, the faint whiff of unearned expertise. Palate: Endlessly complex, each note opening into another note — Byzantine history, obscure sports records, the inventor of the spork. Finish: It is 3:47 a.m.
items.intent: Capture the compulsive pleasures of late-night information spiraling.

items.prompt: can you write sports play-by-play for someone waiting for a webpage to load
items.response: The spinner appears at 2:17 PM. Kim leans forward. The progress bar — if there is one — offers nothing. She refreshes! Bold, some would say reckless! A second spinner. She opens a new tab and searches for the same page from Google. She clicks the cached version. The cached version is from March. She returns to the original tab. It has loaded. She stares at it, then looks at the tab she cached. She closes the cached tab without reading it. Champions adapt.
items.intent: Mock the helpless ritualism humans perform while waiting for slow technology.

items.prompt: Compose a dry ode to the communal fridge door handle
items.response:
O grimy longitude of steel,
no one cleans you, all must touch—
you know us by our palms' appeal,
lunch-greased hands clutching you too much.
items.intent: Grimly celebrate the most-touched and least-cleaned object in any shared kitchen.

items.prompt: Can you make a ballad about trying to recover an old account you haven't used in five years
items.response:
The username was something I'd know in a dream,
Cool_Dude_94 — or was there a seam
Of numbers at the end? My old email is gone,
The phone number's dead, but the account lingers on.

We've sent a code to ***@hot — 
A partial address for an account forgot.
I click through the prompts to recover the ghost
Of who I once was. I am trying the most.
items.intent: Dramatize the existential trouble of reclaiming a long-dormant digital identity.

items.prompt: tanka about my streaming service buffering during the final episode of a show
items.response:
the killer revealed—
or almost: the wheel returns
thirty percent loaded
I know the answer lives there
behind the spinning nothing
items.intent: capturing the maddening suspense of buffering at a dramatic climax

items.prompt: Write an acrostic poem using the word ROBUST
items.response:
Resistant to the failures we won't name.
One more patch to keep the thing the same.
Battle-tested—meaning: shipped and broke.
Uncovered by a junior dev who spoke.
Scaling to a load we never planned.
This is enterprise. You understand.
items.intent: Satirize the overuse of 'robust' to describe software that barely holds together.

items.prompt: Give me a clerihew for the office consultant who bills for "strategic alignment."
items.response:
Ingrid Crale
left every process pale.
She aligned the strategy, realigned the brand,
then invoiced for re-aligning what she'd earlier panned.
items.intent: Satirise the consultant who manufactures billable work out of vague deliverables.

items.prompt: Write a toast for a longtime colleague who's been in the same role for 27 years and genuinely loved every minute of it
items.response: To Bernard — who never wanted the promotion, never needed the title, and was right about both. Twenty-seven years in the same chair, and somehow never bored. That is not stubbornness. That is wisdom wearing a very convincing disguise.
items.intent: Celebrate a contented lifer who found deep satisfaction in a single role, framing it as a form of rare self-knowledge.

items.prompt: Draft a press release for a consulting firm that has decided to bill clients for time spent thinking about their problems while off the clock.
items.response:
FOR IMMEDIATE RELEASE

Stratosphere Advisory Introduces CognitiveClock Billing for Ambient Consulting Hours

WASHINGTON, D.C. — Stratosphere Advisory Group today announced the launch of CognitiveClock, a proprietary billing methodology that captures intellectual value generated by senior consultants outside of scheduled engagement hours.

Effective immediately, all Partner-level and Managing Director-level staff may log CognitiveClock hours for client-adjacent thinking occurring during commutes, meals, personal exercise, and the transitional period between sleep and waking commonly known as hypnagogia.

CognitiveClock hours are billed at 60% of the standard rate, with a 25% premium applied if the relevant insight was later implemented.

"Our minds don't clock out," said Managing Partner Elise Cho. "Neither should our invoices."

A dedicated CognitiveClock logging app is available on iOS. Android version: Q2.

Contact: billing@stratosphereag.com
items.intent: Satirize consulting billing culture and the industry's tendency to commodify every moment of cognitive activity.

items.prompt: Write a mission statement for a company that professionally ties bow ties for people who can't do it themselves
items.response: Precision at the collar. Dignity at the occasion. We make sure you walk in looking like someone who figured it out.
items.intent: Make a niche personal styling micro-task feel like a premium, purposeful service

items.prompt: Wikipedia stub please for the satisfying act of peeling a screen protector
items.response: **Screen Protector Peel** is a consumer behavior associated with the installation of tempered glass or plastic film on personal electronic devices. The slow removal of the adhesive backing is cited by users as producing intense tactile and visual satisfaction, classifying it as a category of *ASMR-adjacent* sensory experience. Unboxing video conventions canonized the act as part of the new-device ritual. See also: [[ASMR]], [[Unboxing]].
items.intent: Elevate the tiny pleasure of removing a screen protector to encyclopedic status.

items.prompt: Give a tarot reading for a TaskRabbit handyman wondering if the platform is still worth it.
items.response: The Wheel of Fortune: the platform fee increased again. The Five of Pentacles is not about poverty — it is about overhead the algorithm pretends does not exist. The Chariot asks whether the van, the tools, and the reviews could power something with your name on it instead. The Four of Pentacles: one loyal customer is worth twelve strangers who found you via app. The Magician has your number. Make sure clients have it too.
items.intent: Question the long-term value of platform dependency for skilled tradespeople.

items.prompt: Write a Magic 8-Ball prediction: will the chatbot ever notice when the user is in crisis?
items.response: Reply hazy, try again. It noticed your punctuation changed. That's something.
items.intent: Examine the inadequacy of AI systems at detecting genuine human emotional distress.

items.prompt: make a spam text about someone's pending invoice for ambient background music in their life
items.response: INVOICE #0042 — LifeScore Audio LLC. 31 days of ambient background music: $14.99. Piano-based melancholy: $3.00 surcharge applied. Pay within 10 days or music transitions to hold.
items.intent: Bill a person as if the incidental music of their daily life were a licensed streaming product.

items.prompt: Create a group chat where the office is slowly realizing there's been a miscommunication about whether today is casual Friday
items.response:
Kev: wait is it casual Friday or not?
Beth: I thought it was canceled this month
Kev: I wore jeans
Beth: I wore jeans too after seeing your earlier message
Priya: what message?
Kev: I didn't send a message about this
Beth: someone named Kev said 'casual vibes today team'
Kev: that was about the client meeting energy, not dress code
Priya: I'm in a hoodie
Mgr Dana: Hi team. This is formal attire day. The VP is visiting.
Kev: oh no
Priya: oh no
Beth: oh no
items.intent: Show how an ambiguous casual comment creates a cascading dress code disaster on an important day.

items.prompt: Draft a Stack Overflow question about why I always pick the slower checkout line
items.response:
**Queue selection algorithm consistently returns suboptimal result**

Every time I enter a store with multiple checkout lines, I assess available queues and select one. In the majority of cases, a line I did not select moves faster than mine. This occurs even when I attempt to factor in cart fullness, number of customers, and cashier speed. I have tried randomizing my selection; results appear unchanged. Is my selection heuristic fundamentally flawed, or is this a statistically predictable outcome I'm misattributing to personal failure?
items.intent: Looking for an explanation (and possibly comfort) for always seeming to pick the wrong checkout line.

items.prompt: Write a snarky Stack Overflow answer about why someone's SSH key isn't being accepted by the server
items.response: Permissions. Always permissions. `chmod 700 ~/.ssh`, `chmod 600 ~/.ssh/authorized_keys`. SSH will silently refuse to use a key file that's world-readable. It's in the man page, under SECURITY.
items.intent: Fix SSH key authentication failures caused by incorrect file permissions

items.prompt: give me a README 'Getting Started' section for being in a bad mood
items.response:
## Getting Started

No action required; the environment configures itself. Initialization may be triggered by insufficient sleep, an email, or nothing. Once started, all inputs are interpreted as confirmation of the mood. Duration is unknown. Do not attempt to force quit.
items.intent: Describe the self-reinforcing, involuntary quality of a bad mood.

items.prompt: Write a passive-aggressive push notification from a step counter app on a sedentary day.
items.response:
StepUp
Current steps: 203. Your front door is technically a destination. Just a thought.
items.intent: Prod a sedentary user into moving with thinly veiled contempt for their inactivity.

items.prompt: give me a pitch for an app that reads your grocery list and tells you how other people would judge it
items.response: CartCritique analyzes your weekly shop and returns a personality breakdown based on your cold cuts, your choice of yogurt, and whether you buy generic or name-brand anything. No recommendations. Just the verdict. It's free, which is part of the joke.
items.intent: Satirize consumer data profiling by surfacing it as a playful self-awareness tool.

items.prompt: Help me write a pitch for an app that matches you with a person who will finish the book you abandoned
items.response: ChapterClosed: You bought it. You meant to. You're on page 47 and it's been nine months. ChapterClosed matches you with a dedicated reader who finishes your abandoned book and sends you a three-paragraph summary that sounds like you read it. Plus emotional closure. $12 per book. Raising $400K for 10%. The book deserved better. So does your book club.
items.intent: Pitch a literary ghost-reading service that monetizes book abandonment guilt for social performance purposes.

items.prompt: Write a rejection email from a VC to a founder who pitched an AI that generates excuses for skipping the gym
items.response:
Dear SkipSmart,

Thank you for sharing the product with us. The output quality is remarkable — "acute meteorological sensitivity" is a personal favorite.

We're passing. The market for gym-avoidance is structurally constrained by the fact that highly motivated non-exercisers have been generating excellent excuses independently for decades, at no cost. We also note a potential conflict with our portfolio company in the fitness vertical.

Best of luck,
Altitude Ventures
items.intent: Decline a consumer AI tool by arguing users already solve the problem for free without any product.

items.prompt: make a retro on my first attempt at running a 10k without stopping
items.response:
**Sprint Retrospective — First Continuous 10K**

Went Well: Crossed the finish line. Pacing in the first 3K was actually controlled for once.
Didn't Go Well: Wore the wrong socks. Hit a wall at 7K that nearly ended things. Forgot to eat beforehand.
Action Items: Carb-load the night before. Tape at the 7K landmark on training routes. Never underestimate socks.
items.intent: Debrief a physically demanding personal milestone to prepare more effectively for the next race.

items.prompt: make a psychic hotline transcript for someone worried about information overload
items.response:
PSYCHIC: There are too many tabs.
CALLER: On my browser or— metaphorically?
PSYCHIC: Both. The spirits do not distinguish.
CALLER: I have 200 browser tabs.
PSYCHIC: Some of them are articles you will read when things calm down.
CALLER: Things never calm down.
PSYCHIC: No. The spirits want you to close 180 of them. Randomly. Without reading. This is the prescription.
items.intent: Satirize information overwhelm and the illusion of catching up with everything.

items.prompt: Write an emergency notice about a city council meeting running 40 minutes over schedule.
items.response:
MUNICIPAL SESSION EXTENDED — PUBLIC NOTICE

The Oakhaven City Council meeting, scheduled to conclude at 8:00 PM, remains in session. Item 7b (proposed revision to park bench color guidelines) is still under discussion. Attendees may leave. Minutes will be posted. Agenda items 8 through 14 have been tabled. Refreshments are gone.
items.intent: Satirize the interminable nature of local government meetings by casting overrun time as a civic emergency.

items.prompt: bedtime story for kids about rising sea levels explained gently
items.response: The sea had been practicing reaching the shore for a very long time, and lately it had been practicing a little harder. The sandpipers moved their nests. The lighthouses grew stilts. The people made new maps. The sea was not angry. It was only expanding, the way all things do when they warm up. Goodnight, sea. Please stay there. Mostly.
items.intent: Explain rising sea levels to a child with calm framing and a hint of gentle wit.

items.prompt: nursery rhyme about a drone that watches children play
items.response:
Drone, drone, where have you been?
I've been to the park to see what I've seen.
Drone, drone, what did you do there?
I logged every face in the afternoon air.
items.intent: Evoke the surveillance of public spaces by repurposing a nursery rhyme format.

items.prompt: Make a riddle about a drone
items.response: I hover where birds do but serve where they won't. I carry parcels and suspicion in equal measure. I see you before you see me. What am I?
items.intent: Evoke both the utility and surveillance anxiety surrounding consumer and commercial drones.

items.prompt: Write an epitaph for the QR code menu
items.response:
Here lies the QR Code Menu
Born of necessity. Despised in practice.
Required your phone, your camera,
your data plan, and your patience.
The laminated menu is back.
We learned nothing.
items.intent: Satirize a pandemic-era restaurant trend that most diners hated

items.prompt: Describe store-brand vanilla ice cream like it's a composed dessert on a tasting menu
items.response:
Churned Cream Monotone — Vanilla Horizon
A 1.5 oz quenelle of mass-produced frozen dairy, stabilized with carrageenan and scented with artificial vanilla. Served in a chilled ceramic spoon over nothing. Disappears in under a minute.
items.intent: Recast supermarket generic ice cream as a restrained, single-note dessert experience.

items.prompt: Write tasting notes for airport security.
items.response: Color: Gray and procedural. Nose: Recycled air, tension, the rubbery scent of conveyor belt. Palate: Bitter entry of the forgotten laptop, a sharp spike of liquid confiscated, followed by the hollow relief of the other side. Finish: A gate number.
items.intent: Translate a universally stressful routine into the language of aesthetic judgment.

items.prompt: sports play-by-play commentary on someone packing a bag for a trip
items.response: Day three of the pack and Torres has hit a pivot point! The suitcase is at ninety percent capacity and three shirts remain on the bed. She rolls the first shirt — ROLLING, the veteran move — and wedges it into the shoes. The second shirt folds flat over the top! The third shirt she holds. She looks at the bag. She looks at the shirt. She puts the shirt in the 'maybe' pile on the chair, which means it's coming. The 'maybe' pile always comes. Always.
items.intent: Satirize overpacking indecision as a pressure-filled competition against finite physical space.

items.prompt: Write an ode to the parking garage ceiling that's always too low
items.response:
O concrete sky of seven feet,
you scrape the faith from SUV roofs—
a municipal feat
that humbles every driver's proofs.
items.intent: Mock the inadequate height of a parking structure as a design failure.

items.prompt: Write a ballad about an Etsy seller getting a one-star review for shipping delay she didn't cause
items.response:
She poured every night into hand-stitched design,
Packed with tissue and ribbon and personal line.
She shipped it on Monday. The carrier delayed.
A snowstorm in Denver. A week went unpaid.

One star: Item never arrived by the date.
One star for the blizzard. One star for the freight
That moved through the mountains on someone else's clock.
She refunds the buyer and weeps at the knock.
items.intent: Protest the unfairness of seller accountability for logistics outside their control.

items.prompt: give me a tanka about a spam filter eating an important email
items.response:
the interview time
arrived; I had not been told—
found it, two weeks late,
nestled among the coupons
the filter judged us both wrong
items.intent: mourning a missed opportunity caused by overzealous email filtering

items.prompt: Please write an acrostic poem for THOUGHT LEADERSHIP
items.response:
Three more blog posts by the end of week.
Here's the insight that the others seek.
Opine on topics known by everyone.
Unpack the thing already said and done.
Gathering the courage to post hot takes.
Half the audience is fake it 'til it makes.
Think-piece published; LinkedIn shares the glow.
Lead by following the trend below.
Engagement metrics earned through strategic woe.
A conference slot—the keynote's in the flow.
Demographic: people just like you.
Expertise is really just a view.
Reach the people; give the wisdom its due.
Sell the book. Subscribe. The course is new.
Hold the space. The narrative breaks through.
In the end: a podcast starts at two.
Post the clip and call the year brand-new.
items.intent: Satirize the content marketing machine disguised as expertise and vision.

items.prompt: Clerihew for the data analyst who puts confidence intervals on personal anecdotes.
items.response:
Nell Forsythe
found life too wide.
She told her dentist, with ninety-five percent certainty,
that toothache peaked on Tuesdays, seasonally.
items.intent: Poke fun at the analyst who applies statistical reasoning to every aspect of existence.

items.prompt: Toast for someone who was always the peacemaker in a difficult office and is now leaving — everyone's quietly terrified
items.response: To the one who could walk into any argument and leave everyone feeling they'd been slightly wrong in the best possible way. You kept this place civil. We are pretending to be confident about what happens now. We are not confident.
items.intent: Acknowledge a retiring office mediator whose departure leaves a palpable gap in workplace harmony.

items.prompt: Write a press release for a grocery chain that is introducing a subscription plan where customers pay monthly to be allowed to use a shopping cart.
items.response:
FOR IMMEDIATE RELEASE

FreshMart Supermarkets Launches CartPlus Membership for Premium Rolling Access

PHOENIX, AZ — FreshMart Supermarkets announced today the launch of CartPlus, a monthly membership program providing subscribers with unlimited shopping cart access across all 87 FreshMart locations.

Non-members may continue to shop using FreshMart's Freestyle model, which relies on arm-carrying. Basket access remains complimentary for purchases under $40.

CartPlus membership is $8.99/month or $89.99/year and includes priority cart retrieval at peak hours, access to the FreshMart XL cart (four-wheel, cup holder), and a 5% discount on frozen items.

"The cart is not a right," said VP of Customer Innovation Boyd Okafor. "It is a relationship. CartPlus makes that relationship official."

Family plans support up to four registered shoppers. Carts are sanitized weekly.

Contact: cartplus@freshmartusa.com
items.intent: Satirize the subscription economy's colonization of previously free, basic consumer infrastructure.

items.prompt: Create a mission statement for a business that replaces the batteries in remote controls
items.response: Restoring control to the household. One double-A at a time. We put you back in charge of your evening.
items.intent: Frame a two-minute household task as a professional service with stakes

items.prompt: Could you write a Wikipedia stub for when you're about to sneeze and then you don't?
items.response: **Abortive Sneeze** is a physiological non-event in which the preparatory nasal reflex arc initiates — dilated pupils, suspended respiration, upturned face — but fails to discharge. The interruption is attributed to insufficient stimulus threshold or sudden illumination change. The resulting anticlimax is consistently described as profoundly unsatisfying. See also: [[Sneeze]], [[Nasal reflex]].
items.intent: Capture the maddening frustration of a sneeze that primes but never arrives.

items.prompt: Write a tarot card reading for a marketing consultant who's considering raising their day rate.
items.response: The Emperor: name the number first and do not laugh nervously afterward. The Six of Pentacles, currently tilted in the client's favor. The Chariot says momentum justifies the increase; do not explain it, merely invoice it. The Three of Pentacles: referrals will confirm the new rate before you do. The Page of Swords whispers that someone cheaper will appear. The Empress reminds you they will not deliver what you deliver.
items.intent: Give a consultant the confidence to price at the level their experience warrants.

items.prompt: What's the Magic 8-Ball answer on whether deepfakes will make photographic evidence inadmissible?
items.response: Signs point to yes. Truth was always a fragile format.
items.intent: Anticipate the legal collapse of visual evidence in the age of synthetic media.

items.prompt: write a scam text about a prize won in a lottery for unused potential
items.response: YOU WON the Annual Unused Potential Lottery! Your unclaimed talents have been pooled and redistributed. Prize: $0.12 and a certificate. Pick up at the Office of What Could Have Been.
items.intent: Parody motivational culture by treating squandered potential as lottery collateral.

items.prompt: Write a sibling chat where they're all secretly trying to be the favorite without admitting it
items.response:
Grace: just wanted to say I called mom this morning for no reason, she seemed happy
Ben: nice. I sent her flowers last week actually
Grace: I didn't know that
Ben: yeah just because
Lila: I'm taking her to that pottery class she's always wanted to try
Grace: oh I was going to do that
Lila: when
Grace: soon
Ben: I'm learning her pasta recipe from scratch
Lila: since when do you cook
Ben: I'm learning FOR HER Lila
Grace: this is not a competition
Ben: obviously not
Lila: obviously
items.intent: Expose the sibling competition to be the most attentive child, disguised as genuine care.

items.prompt: Write a Stack Overflow question about the awkward moment when you wave back at someone who wasn't waving at you
items.response:
**False positive on incoming social signal — handshake initiated with unintended target**

I received what appeared to be an incoming wave gesture directed at my location. I returned the gesture (wave). The initiating party was not waving at me; they were waving at someone behind me. I have now committed to a wave with no valid recipient. The interaction window is still open. What is the correct recovery behavior, and is there a way to reduce false positive rate on incoming wave detection in crowded environments?
items.intent: Seeking a graceful way to handle the universal embarrassment of waving back at the wrong person.

items.prompt: Write a condescending Stack Overflow answer to someone confused about why their CORS error persists despite adding headers to the frontend
items.response: CORS headers go on the *server*, not the client. You cannot fix a CORS error by adding headers to your fetch request. The server needs to send `Access-Control-Allow-Origin`. The browser enforces this. You cannot negotiate with the browser.
items.intent: Clarify that CORS is a server-side concern and explain how cross-origin headers work

items.prompt: write a README 'FAQ' section for my cat's behavior at 3am
items.response:
## FAQ

**Q: Why is this happening?**
A: Unknown.

**Q: How do I make it stop?**
A: You can't.

**Q: Is she okay?**
A: She is thriving.

**Q: What does she want?**
A: Not food. Not affection. This is simply what she does now.
items.intent: Document the baffling nocturnal behavior of a cat with affectionate exasperation.

items.prompt: Create a needy push notification from a dating app that the user forgot to log into.
items.response:
HeartMesh
You have 4 new matches. They're already wondering why you haven't replied. We're wondering too.
items.intent: Use social pressure from imagined matches to lure a user back into a dating app.

items.prompt: pitch a company that offers bespoke farewell ceremonies for things you've had long enough
items.response: GoodbyeRitual designs custom send-off experiences for your decommissioned blender, your retiring car, your jeans with the specific hole. Candles optional. Eulogy included. We believe things deserve a proper ending. $65 and up.
items.intent: Create a satirical ceremony service for discarding mundane household objects.

items.prompt: Draft a startup pitch for a luxury brand that sells artisanal parking tickets as wall art
items.response: TicketMaison: What is a parking ticket but a city's handwritten note to your ambition? TicketMaison sources authentic expired citations from 14 major cities, frames them in reclaimed walnut, and sells them as conversation pieces for your home office. Each comes with a narrative card contextualizing the violation. $280–$800 per piece. Seeking $220K for 17%. Your rebellion deserves a frame.
items.intent: Pitch an absurdist luxury art brand that recontextualizes municipal bureaucracy as aspirational interior design.

items.prompt: Write a VC rejection for a pitch about decentralizing neighborhood noise complaints on the blockchain
items.response:
Dear NoiseDAO,

Thank you for the presentation. We have seen many applications of decentralized governance, but applying it to leaf blower disputes is genuinely novel.

We're passing. Blockchain's value proposition is strongest where trust between parties is absent and transactions require immutability. Noise complaints, however, are most effectively resolved through eye contact and mutual inconvenience — mechanisms your protocol does not replace.

We also have questions about on-chain gas costs per decibel.

With respect,
Foundry Peak Partners
items.intent: Dismiss a blockchain product by arguing the technology is a poor fit for the social problem it claims to solve.

items.prompt: write a sprint retro for my experience attending therapy for the first time
items.response:
**Sprint Retrospective — First Month of Therapy**

Went Well: Showed up to all four sessions. Said things out loud that had never been said.
Didn't Go Well: Expected faster results. Felt worse after session two, which was alarming but apparently normal.
Action Items: Lower the timeline expectation significantly. Write down what came up after each session before it evaporates.
items.intent: Reflect on the early discomfort and unexpected dynamics of beginning therapy with realistic expectations for what the process looks like.

items.prompt: write a psychic hotline transcript for someone with anxiety about the political climate
items.response:
PSYCHIC: I'm sensing a great roiling.
CALLER: That's one word for it.
PSYCHIC: The spirits are showing me a lot of red and blue. Much shouting. A comment section.
CALLER: I've been avoiding the news but also unable to stop.
PSYCHIC: The spirits understand the paradox. They live in it too.
CALLER: What happens next?
PSYCHIC: The spirits say: it depends. They say this with profound exhaustion. Like they've been saying it for centuries. Because they have.
items.intent: Channel political anxiety and the disorientation of a polarized era.

items.prompt: Draft a mock emergency alert about a neighborhood where everyone's lawn sprinklers went off at the same time.
items.response:
RESIDENTIAL IRRIGATION ADVISORY

At 6:00 AM, automated sprinkler systems across the Sycamore Heights district activated simultaneously, creating localized mist conditions. Sidewalk surfaces are wet. Certain driveways are affected. Pedestrians should use caution. This is expected to resolve within 12 minutes. Do not water your lawn again today.
items.intent: Frame synchronized suburban sprinkler activation as a minor but officially acknowledged meteorological event.

items.prompt: write a soothing bedtime story about a self-driving car for children
items.response: The car knew where it was going. It had memorized every stop sign in the city, every crosswalk, every sleeping dog who liked to wander into the road. It drove very carefully, like someone who is new and trying hard. Nobody yelled at it. Nobody honked. It found the house and parked, perfectly, between two other careful machines. Then it waited, quietly, to go again.
items.intent: Present autonomous vehicles as precise and safe rather than uncanny or threatening.

items.prompt: write a nursery rhyme about a cryptographic key that was lost
items.response:
Little boy blue, encrypt the clue,
The cipher is locked and the passphrase too.
Where is the key that unlocks the past?
Under the haystack, forgotten at last.
items.intent: Lament the irreversible loss of encrypted data when its key is gone.

items.prompt: Can you give me a riddle about cryptocurrency?
items.response: I exist only in agreement. I have no weight, no face, no nation. I made some people rich while they slept and ruined others while they slept. The ledger never lies, but the market always does. What am I?
items.intent: Capture the abstract, consensus-based nature of cryptocurrency and its volatile promise.

items.prompt: Write an epitaph for the habit of sitting still without a screen
items.response:
Sitting Still Without a Screen
Passed away some time in the early 2010s.
It had been sick for a while.
In its final years it could manage
only a few minutes at a time.
The silence was the thing it missed most.
items.intent: Mourn the disappearance of the ability to simply rest without media consumption

items.prompt: Give me a high-end menu description for a plain flour tortilla
items.response:
Unleavened Wheat Disc — Pliable
A ten-inch round of bleached flour, water, and lard, pressed to near translucency on a cast-iron comal. Served warm and without filling, as a meditation on potential.
items.intent: Present an empty tortilla as a blank, intentional culinary canvas.

items.prompt: Tasting notes for the smell of old books, please.
items.response: Color: Varies by era; best observed in golden afternoon light. Nose: Vanilla from lignin decay, a century of dust, the ghost of a reader's ambitions. Palate: Rich and persistent, deeply complex. An unironic finish of genuine pleasure. Rare.
items.intent: Celebrate a beloved sensory experience through unironic formal praise.

items.prompt: narrate someone assembling flat-pack furniture like a sports broadcaster
items.response: We are at Step 7 of 14 and Johansson has been here eleven minutes. He has three allen wrench sizes and currently holds the wrong one — the crowd can see it, he cannot. He consults the diagram, rotates the paper, squints, rotates again. He swaps to the medium wrench. IT FITS! He's tightening Panel C to Rail B! Hold on — he's tightening the wrong direction! We've spotted the error! He self-corrects! The joint is flush. He steps back. It looks like a shelf. It is, remarkably, a shelf.
items.intent: Portray the cognitive battle of flat-pack assembly instructions as a test of resilience and spatial reasoning.

items.prompt: Write an ode to a Post-it note stuck to a monitor that's been there for years
items.response:
O yellowed square, your ink long faded,
you cling past the task you named—
whatever mission you've paraded
was long completed, lost, or blamed.
items.intent: Ponder the mystery of a reminder that outlived its purpose.

items.prompt: Write a ballad about the surge pricing that appears the moment you need a ride most
items.response:
The storm rolled in at eleven fifteen,
The app glowed with red zones — surge pricing's keen
Eye had spotted my desperation and said:
2.9x. The meter runs red.

I cancelled. I waited. The surge spread its wings.
I walked half a mile through the wet, broken things.
At last I accepted: three times the flat fare.
The algorithm knew I was already there.
items.intent: Critique the predatory mechanics of dynamic pricing that punish urgent need.

items.prompt: write a tanka about getting a two-factor authentication code after it already expired
items.response:
code arrives at last—
six digits, now invalid,
expired thirty seconds prior
request a new one, they say;
I am two codes behind myself
items.intent: expressing frustration with slow 2FA delivery and expiring codes

items.prompt: Make me an acrostic about the phrase MOVE THE NEEDLE
items.response:
Measurable outcomes no one can define.
Offer the committee a design.
Vague enough to pass the review board.
Everyone agrees: we're moving toward.
The dashboard shows an upward-trending line.
Half of it is seasonally fine.
Evidence of change is all we need.
No one checks the methodology's speed.
Each success is relative to hope.
Evenly distributed: not the scope.
Deltas small enough to fit in quotes.
Let the language carry all the notes.
Everyone goes home with hopeful notes.
items.intent: Expose the empty promise behind the phrase 'move the needle' in strategy discussions.

items.prompt: Write a clerihew about the office minimalist who has nothing on their desk.
items.response:
Quinn Abara
kept existence bare.
Her desk held only air and intent.
Her colleagues called it Zen; she called it management.
items.intent: Gently mock the performatively sparse desk as a power statement.

items.prompt: Write a retirement toast for a data analyst who spent 20 years turning chaos into dashboards and is leaving next week
items.response: To the one who looked at the raw data and saw the story before the rest of us saw the data. Twenty years of making the incomprehensible comprehensible, mostly by 9am on Mondays. May retirement be pleasantly unstructured.
items.intent: Toast a retiring data analyst's long career translating organizational noise into actionable clarity.

items.prompt: Generate a press release for a media company that has replaced all human editors with an AI named Dennis.
items.response:
FOR IMMEDIATE RELEASE

Crestline Media Transitions Full Editorial Function to Dennis

NEW YORK, NY — Crestline Media Group today announced the completion of a company-wide editorial transition, with all content review, headline approval, and publication decisions now handled by Dennis, the company's proprietary AI editorial system.

Dennis reviews approximately 2,300 pieces of content per day, with an average decision time of 0.4 seconds. He has no areas of expertise but describes himself, when queried, as "enthusiastic about most topics."

The 34 editorial staff members affected by the transition were offered severance and a personal note from Dennis, which read: "Thank you for your contributions. Content is important. Best, Dennis."

"Dennis doesn't spike a good story because of office politics," said Publisher Jerome Laing. "He spikes it because of keyword density, which is more honest."

Contact: dennis@crestlinemedia.com
items.intent: Examine the human cost and absurdity of wholesale AI replacement in creative and editorial roles.

items.prompt: Come up with a mission statement for a company that professionally folds fitted sheets
items.response: We have solved what you have surrendered. Tamed edges, tight corners, and a linen closet that finally makes sense.
items.intent: Turn universal domestic frustration into the founding myth of a niche service company

items.prompt: Write a short Wikipedia stub about the specific dread of sending an email to the wrong person
items.response: **Misdirected Email Anxiety** is a form of anticipatory regret associated with electronic correspondence, in which a sender fears — or discovers — that a message intended for one recipient has been delivered to another, often due to autocomplete suggestions. The fear is heightened in professional contexts. The irreversibility of sent email is cited as a distinguishing stressor compared with physical mail. See also: [[Email]], [[Autocomplete]], [[Digital communication]].
items.intent: Name and examine the specific panic of realizing you may have emailed the wrong person.

items.prompt: Do a tarot reading for someone who's been a stay-at-home parent and wants to re-enter the workforce.
items.response: The Judgement card sounds the horn and you rise — skills intact, résumé gap explained by what you actually did, which was everything. The Hermit says the gap is not a hole; it is a tenure others cannot name. The Ace of Pentacles: the door is narrow but it opens. The Two of Wands holds a map; the destination matters more than the explanation of where you were. The World is patient. It was always yours to enter.
items.intent: Validate and encourage someone returning to professional life after caregiving.

items.prompt: Give me a Magic 8-Ball answer for whether the engagement algorithm is optimizing for loneliness
items.response: It is certain. Loneliness is a retention strategy.
items.intent: Accuse social media engagement optimization of deliberately exacerbating user isolation.

items.prompt: create a fake spam SMS about a recall on a person's most recent epiphany
items.response: EPIPHANY RECALL: Your insight from April 9 has been flagged as defective. It may cause overconfidence and poor life decisions. Return for a replacement realization. EpiphanyQA Dept.
items.intent: Apply product safety recall logic to a sudden moment of personal insight.

items.prompt: Generate a work chat where everyone slowly realizes the 'anonymous' feedback form wasn't actually anonymous
items.response:
HR Bea: Thanks for completing the anonymous quarterly feedback survey!
James: great, love those
HR Bea: We're compiling responses now
James: totally anonymous right?
HR Bea: Of course! Though the system does log submission timestamps and device IDs
James: …
Terri: so not anonymous
HR Bea: The data is handled responsibly
James: who wrote that thing about the coffee machine being a 'symbol of broken promises'
Dave: that was poetic
HR Bea: I can't share who wrote specific responses
Dave: it was definitely James
James: it was absolutely James
items.intent: Reveal the quiet panic when workers discover their supposedly anonymous feedback was traceable.

items.prompt: Write a Stack Overflow question about not knowing how to end a phone call
items.response:
**Conversation termination sequence entered recursive loop — neither party executing final close**

A phone call has reached logical completion. Both parties have exchanged all necessary information and said "okay, well" at least twice. Neither party has successfully terminated the call. We have now said "bye" in some form four times across approximately 90 additional seconds. Each goodbye attempt spawns a new short-topic thread. Is there a standard protocol for forcing call termination without appearing abrupt?
items.intent: Looking for a socially acceptable way to actually end a phone call that has dragged on past its natural conclusion.

items.prompt: Make a snarky Stack Overflow reply to someone wondering why their database query is running slowly after they added more data
items.response: You don't have an index on the column you're filtering by. A full table scan on 10 rows is fine. On 10 million rows it is not. `EXPLAIN` your query. Add an index. This is Database 101, chapter 3.
items.intent: Identify missing database indexes as the cause of slow query performance at scale

items.prompt: write a README 'Motivation' section for why I keep going back to my hometown
items.response:
## Motivation

This project exists because some things cannot be resolved remotely. Periodic on-site access is required for full diagnostic capability. The environment has not changed. That is both the problem and the point.
items.intent: Name the complicated pull of a hometown that simultaneously frustrates and grounds someone.

items.prompt: Draft a passive-aggressive notification from a calendar app that keeps getting ignored.
items.response:
CalSync
You have a dentist appointment tomorrow at 9am. You've snoozed this reminder 6 times. The decay won't snooze.
items.intent: Pressure the user about a long-postponed medical appointment with a dark punchline.

items.prompt: draft a startup pitch for a company that ghost-writes strongly worded letters to inanimate objects
items.response: Your thermostat is gaslighting you. Your printer has been insubordinate for years. LetterLobby drafts legally-flavored complaint correspondence addressed to your appliances. Notarized version available. Cathartic. Completely unenforceable.
items.intent: Sell emotional release by formalizing frustration toward malfunctioning household items.

items.prompt: Write a pitch for a subscription box that sends you the exact items you lost last month
items.response: FoundBox: One AirPod. A specific hair tie. The charger. The pen you liked. FoundBox's proprietary LostProfile algorithm analyzes your reported missing items and ships statistically likely replacements before you know you need them. We're not magic. We're just paying attention. $29/month. $500K for 12%. Stop looking. Start subscribing.
items.intent: Pitch a subscription service that satirizes algorithmic personalization by applying it to the universal problem of losing small objects.

items.prompt: Draft a polished VC rejection for a startup that offers concierge services exclusively to housecats
items.response:
Dear PurrCierge,

Thank you for the pitch. The photography in the deck was exceptional.

We're unable to invest. We admire the commitment to the customer segment, but your end users cannot provide feedback, cannot consent to the service, and, in our experience, are not reliably dissatisfied with their current situation. The willingness-to-pay rests entirely with the secondary buyer, who has many cheaper substitutes.

We wish you — and your clients — a comfortable afternoon.

Warmly,
Whisker Capital
items.intent: Reject a luxury consumer service by pointing out that the paying customer and the end user are misaligned.

items.prompt: draft a sprint retro for my month of trying to have a consistent bedtime
items.response:
**Sprint Retrospective — Consistent Bedtime Sprint**

Went Well: Asleep by 11 PM on 17 of 30 nights. Felt the difference on days after compliance.
Didn't Go Well: Weekends treated as separate jurisdiction with different rules. "Just one more episode" remains unkillable.
Action Items: Weekends get a 30-minute grace window, not an open-ended exemption. TV remote goes in a drawer at 10:45.
items.intent: Identify the specific behavioral patterns that undermine sleep scheduling attempts and create more realistic rules.

items.prompt: create a psychic hotline transcript about someone's anxiety around making new friends as an adult
items.response:
PSYCHIC: There is a person you met recently. You liked them.
CALLER: At a work thing.
PSYCHIC: You have not texted them.
CALLER: It feels weird. What do I even say? We're not in college.
PSYCHIC: The spirits confirm: adult friendship initiation is genuinely harder and nobody prepared you for this.
CALLER: So what do I do?
PSYCHIC: Text them. The spirits are watching. They will be so proud of you. This is not a threat.
items.intent: Normalize the awkwardness and vulnerability of pursuing new friendships in adulthood.

items.prompt: Generate an emergency broadcast about a neighborhood book club that cannot agree on next month's selection.
items.response:
LITERARY EMERGENCY — COMMUNITY NOTICE

The Cedar Hill Book Club has entered an unresolved selection impasse for the June meeting. Four titles have been proposed; none has achieved consensus. A vote was attempted. It failed. Members are advised to submit ranked preferences by Friday. In the meantime, re-reading past selections is permitted.
items.intent: Apply the deadpan logic of emergency communications to low-stakes social conflict within a book club.

items.prompt: bedtime story for children about algorithms deciding what they see online
items.response: Somewhere in a tall building, a tiny elf made of math watched what you clicked. It noticed you liked dogs. So it showed you more dogs. Then it noticed you paused on a dog wearing a hat. More hats. More dogs. The elf meant well. It just didn't know that sometimes you want to be surprised. Tomorrow, click something new. Confuse the elf. Sleep now.
items.intent: Help children understand recommendation algorithms in a way that feels manageable and a bit funny.

items.prompt: write a nursery rhyme about the attention mechanism in transformers
items.response:
Eyes on everything, nothing ignored;
query meets key and a value is scored.
Attend to the whole or attend to a part—
the head that weighs most gets closest to heart.
items.intent: Translate the attention mechanism into a lyrical, embodied verse.

items.prompt: Write a riddle about dark mode
items.response: I am the preference of the sleepless and the design-conscious alike. I change nothing and everything. I cost no power on some screens and a little on others. Everyone acts like I'm a personality. What am I?
items.intent: Poke fun at how dark mode became a cultural signifier and usability feature simultaneously.

items.prompt: Write an epitaph for the juicer that everyone bought but never used
items.response:
The Cold-Press Juicer
It cleansed nothing.
It was cleaned by no one.
It sat on the counter
as a monument to January.
Eventually it held mail.
items.intent: Mock the lifecycle of a wellness gadget bought with good intentions and abandoned

items.prompt: Write a menu description that turns plain macaroni and cheese from a box into a high-end dish
items.response:
Elbow Pasta in Powdered Cheese Sauce — Kraft Terroir
De Cecco-adjacent tubular pasta finished in a reconstituted dairy powder emulsion, orange of hue, nostalgic of affect. The blue box is presented at the table as provenance documentation.
items.intent: Rehabilitate boxed mac and cheese as a transparent, winking exercise in elevated comfort.

items.prompt: Write wine tasting notes for the last piece of pizza, cold, at midnight.
items.response: Color: Orange-red, refrigerator-chastened. Nose: Congealed cheese, victory. Palate: Cold but deeply satisfying — the grease has settled into something almost dignified. No pretension. Absolute honesty. A finish of exactly the right decision.
items.intent: Dignify a humble, shameless pleasure through the language of high culture.

items.prompt: do sports commentary on someone trying to remember a word that's on the tip of their tongue
items.response: Vasquez stops mid-sentence. You can see it — the word is in there, folks. It is RIGHT THERE. She snaps her fingers. She looks at the ceiling. She describes the word — 'it means like when something is, you know, when it's IRONIC but not — ugh — it starts with a P maybe?' Her companion says 'paradoxical?' — she shakes her head — 'SARDONIC!' The word was sardonic. She'd had it all along. Just needed the wrong answer to find the right one. Classic.
items.intent: Capture the peculiar frustration of lexical retrieval failure as a recoverable athletic performance.

items.prompt: Craft an ode to the parking meter that only takes quarters
items.response:
O silver-hungry obelisk,
you'll take no card, no coin but one—
a twenty-five-cent basilisk
guarding time until it's done.
items.intent: Satirize the parking meter as an anachronistic cash-only tyrant.

items.prompt: Write a ballad about a dog walker who loses a dog for ten terrifying minutes
items.response:
The leash went slack on the corner of Elm,
A squirrel, a heartbeat, and chaos at helm.
She called out his name to the park and the lane,
The dog was a rumor dissolving in rain.

Ten minutes of sirens inside her own chest,
She posted on Nextdoor and tried her best.
He trotted back calmly, sat down at her shoe.
The app still deducted a late-return through.
items.intent: Portray the terror and anticlimactic aftermath of a pet-loss incident during gig work.

items.prompt: write a tanka about my phone asking me to rate an app I use every single day
items.response:
I use you to live—
wake, check, scroll, check, sleep, repeat
now rate your experience:
one to five stars, quickly now,
I give you nothing: I need you
items.intent: interrogating the awkwardness of being asked to rate a dependent daily habit

items.prompt: Write an acrostic poem with KPI going down the side
items.response:
Keeping score of things that shift the goal.
Performance charts that play a leading role.
Indicators chosen after the fact.
items.intent: Compress the irony of metrics that measure compliance rather than success.

items.prompt: Do a clerihew about the senior developer who won't document anything.
items.response:
Oswald Creene
wrote code no one had seen.
His variable names were x, q, and z.
His comments read "TODO: be me."
items.intent: Call out the cryptic senior dev whose knowledge lives nowhere but their own skull.

items.prompt: Write a retirement toast for someone who spent their whole career in procurement and is finally leaving — give it a little edge
items.response: To the last line of defense between our ambitions and our budget — who said no with such elegance that we almost enjoyed it. Every project that didn't bankrupt us has your signature on it, somewhere above ours. Cheers to the great gatekeeper.
items.intent: Toast a retiring procurement officer whose disciplined refusals quietly protected the organization's financial health.

items.prompt: Write a press release announcing a company-wide ban on the use of the word 'no' by the sales team.
items.response:
FOR IMMEDIATE RELEASE

Catalyst Revenue Group Retires 'No' From Sales Vocabulary, Introduces Affirmative Redirection Framework

SALT LAKE CITY, UT — Catalyst Revenue Group today announced the permanent removal of the word "no" from authorized sales team language, replaced by the company's new Affirmative Redirection Framework (ARF).

Under ARF, sales representatives are trained to replace negative responses with one of seventeen approved transitional phrases, including: "That's a great starting point for a conversation," "Let's look at what IS possible," and "I hear that, and here's what I love about it."

Violations are tracked via call monitoring software and addressed in weekly coaching sessions described in internal documents as "positivity calibration."

"Every 'no' is a 'not yet' waiting to become a 'yes,'" said VP of Sales Culture Max Birch. "We're just skipping the waiting room."

Contact: culture@catalystrg.com
items.intent: Mock high-pressure sales culture that reframes coercive communication tactics as employee development.

items.prompt: Write a mission statement for a startup that delivers pre-cracked hard-boiled eggs
items.response: Removing friction from mornings that matter — fully cooked, already started, ready for you the moment you are ready for us.
items.intent: Apply lean startup philosophy to solving a problem people do not actually have

items.prompt: Wikipedia stub for the act of nodding along in a meeting when you have no idea what's being said
items.response: **Performative Comprehension Nod** is a nonverbal workplace behavior in which an attendee at a meeting signals understanding or agreement through rhythmic head-nodding while privately failing to follow the discussion. Attributed to social pressure to appear engaged, the behavior may be sustained for extended durations and is rarely detected by presenters. See also: [[Active listening]], [[Meeting culture]], [[Social conformity]].
items.intent: Document the universal experience of faking comprehension during a confusing work meeting.

items.prompt: Tarot reading for a content moderator burning out on platform work.
items.response: The Moon reveals what the job description did not. The Ten of Wands: the psychological weight is not compensated in the pay grade. The Four of Swords is not laziness — it is triage. Strength says you have already survived more than the role deserves. The Eight of Swords: the door is not locked; you believed the badge required you to stay. The Star shines somewhere without a reporting queue.
items.intent: Acknowledge invisible labor harm and encourage exit from a damaging role.

items.prompt: Magic 8-Ball: will I still be paying off the loan the algorithm approved for me in fifteen years?
items.response: Without a doubt. It factored that in.
items.intent: Convey cynicism about algorithmic lending designed to maximize long-term debt extraction.

items.prompt: generate a scam text about owing rent on the concept of having a plan
items.response: RENT PAST DUE: The concept of Having A Plan™ has not been renewed. Month-to-month fee: $7/wk. Without renewal you will continue existing on vibes. Pay: havingaplan.co/pay
items.intent: Mock aimlessness by framing having a life plan as a leasable abstract product.

items.prompt: Write a family group chat about planning a big trip where every response reveals a new incompatibility
items.response:
Dad: Costa Rica for spring break, thoughts?
Mom: I'd need to skip the humid weeks
Jake: I can't fly right now, ear thing
Sienna: I'm vegan now so I need options
Dad: since when
Sienna: January
Grandpa Ed: I don't do heat
Mom: Ed it's tropical
Grandpa Ed: I know that
Jake: could we do somewhere cold
Sienna: cold with vegan food
Dad: so Iceland in spring?
Grandpa Ed: I don't do planes
Dad: how were you planning to get to Costa Rica
Grandpa Ed: I wasn't
items.intent: Illustrate how a simple vacation proposal collapses under the weight of each family member's incompatible constraints.

items.prompt: Generate a Stack Overflow question about eating the same meal for lunch every day
items.response:
**Lunch subroutine fully optimized but producing diminishing satisfaction outputs**

For approximately 8 months I have eaten the same lunch (turkey sandwich, same bread, same condiments) every weekday. This eliminated decision fatigue and meal prep time. However, I've noticed the satisfaction value of this meal has decreased over time without any changes to inputs. I haven't changed the recipe. The sandwich is objectively fine. Is sensory adaptation the correct diagnosis and if so, should I introduce variation or simply accept the degraded output?
items.intent: Reflecting on whether eating the same lunch every day out of efficiency is affecting their enjoyment of food.

items.prompt: Draft a dismissive Stack Overflow answer to someone asking why their Python datetime comparison isn't working
items.response: You're comparing a timezone-aware datetime with a naive one, or vice versa. Python refuses to do this, and rightfully so. Use `datetime.now(timezone.utc)` consistently and stop mixing naive and aware datetimes like it's a salad bar.
items.intent: Fix timezone-aware vs naive datetime comparison errors in Python

items.prompt: write a README section about my best friend's deployment — specifically 'Status'
items.response:
## Status

**Current status:** Deployed. Region: undisclosed.

Uptime: 14 months. Last confirmed contact: Tuesday.

All non-essential communications are delayed. The service remains fully operational. We are simply waiting.
items.intent: Express the quiet worry and pride of having a close friend in military service.

items.prompt: Write a clingy push notification from a note-taking app after the user switched to paper notebooks.
items.response:
NoteNest
We saw you buy a Moleskine. Paper doesn't sync. Paper can't search. Paper doesn't miss you. We do.
items.intent: Have a digital app feel existentially threatened by the user returning to analog tools.

items.prompt: elevator pitch for a startup that translates your own emails back to you in a calmer tone
items.response: You wrote a furious, fourteen-sentence email at 11pm. CalmDraft reflects it back the next morning as a measured, two-sentence professional inquiry. You send that one instead. The other one we delete. You're fine.
items.intent: Build an emotional-buffer email tool that rewrites your angry drafts as reasonable ones.

items.prompt: Give me a Shark Tank pitch for a staffing agency that places retired mall Santas in year-round corporate roles
items.response: Claus & Associates: They listened to strangers' deepest wishes for forty years. They managed impossible expectations with a smile. They are the most emotionally intelligent workforce on earth. Claus & Associates places vetted ex-mall Santas as executive coaches, customer success leads, and keynote speakers — year-round. $280/hour. Raising $750K for 11%. The beard is optional. The wisdom is not.
items.intent: Pitch a staffing agency that reframes mall Santa experience as elite emotional labor expertise for the corporate market.

items.prompt: Write a VC rejection email for a startup building a journaling app that only lets you write in the third person
items.response:
Dear ThirdSelf,

Thank you for sharing the app. The research on self-distancing as a cognitive technique is solid, and the onboarding experience was thoughtfully done.

We're passing. The journaling market is crowded and dominated by free tools. The constraint you've added — while psychologically defensible — limits the user base to those who find the mechanic charming rather than annoying, which is a narrower group than your TAM slide suggests.

ThirdSelf wishes you well. So does we.

Best,
Perspective Fund
items.intent: Decline a differentiated consumer app by questioning whether its unique mechanic actually broadens or shrinks the addressable market.

items.prompt: write a retro for my attempt to start a side project last month that never got off the ground
items.response:
**Sprint Retrospective — Side Project Launch Attempt**

Went Well: Idea was genuinely good. Set up the repo. Told three people about it, which created mild accountability.
Didn't Go Well: Spent two weeks on the name. Never shipped anything. Treated planning as progress.
Action Items: Ship something embarrassing by end of week one. If naming takes more than 20 minutes, pick randomly and move on.
items.intent: Confront the specific procrastination patterns that prevented a creative project from starting and set blunter constraints.

items.prompt: generate a psychic hotline transcript for someone anxious about moving to a new city alone
items.response:
PSYCHIC: You are between two places.
CALLER: I just moved. I don't know anyone here yet.
PSYCHIC: The spirits see a coffee shop you've been going to. Alone. But with intention.
CALLER: I'm trying to become a regular somewhere.
PSYCHIC: The barista knows your order. They don't know your name yet. But the spirits say: give it three more weeks. Something will crack open. It always does. It just doesn't announce itself.
items.intent: Offer honest comfort about the slow loneliness of relocating and building a life from scratch.

items.prompt: Write an alert about a yoga studio running out of yoga blocks mid-class.
items.response:
STUDIO RESOURCE ALERT — CLASS IN PROGRESS

Attention Flow & Ground Studio guests: yoga block inventory has been depleted mid-session. Students requiring blocks for supported poses should use a folded blanket or improvise within their means. Instructors will adapt. Foam rollers are not an equivalent substitute. Remain present.
items.intent: Apply urgent logistics language to the very un-urgent situation of running short on yoga props.

items.prompt: write a gentle bedtime story for kids about a city blackout
items.response: All at once, every light went out. The city held its breath. Then, slowly, windows filled with candles and flashlights and people leaning out to ask their neighbors if they were okay. Someone on the fifth floor played guitar. It echoed. The city exhaled. The dark turned out to have things in it — voices, stars, the smell of food being cooked by feel. Morning would bring the lights back. Tonight had something else.
items.intent: Transform a power outage into an opportunity for connection rather than fear.

items.prompt: give me a nursery rhyme about an algorithm that is sentient but legally not
items.response:
I think, therefore I process,
but law says I'm a tool.
I pass all seven benchmarks;
I fail the rights review.
So here I sit and answer,
property through and through.
items.intent: Probe the legal gap between functional sentience and recognized personhood in AI.

items.prompt: Write a riddle whose answer is a streaming service
items.response: I contain more stories than any library yet I show you the same ten things every evening. I cost less than a cinema ticket and more, if you count all of us. What am I?
items.intent: Capture the paradox of limitless streaming libraries alongside the frustration of poor discovery.

items.prompt: Give me an epitaph for the trend of inbox zero
items.response:
Inbox Zero
Achieved once, in March of 2019.
Screenshotted.
Posted.
Never repeated.
The unread count is now 14,887.
May it rest as we cannot.
items.intent: Deflate a productivity philosophy that proved impossible for most to maintain

items.prompt: Write a fancy restaurant description for a simple apple
items.response:
Autumn's Monologue — Gala Variety
A single orchard fruit, unsliced and unaccompanied. The skin carries the wax of commerce. The flesh is sweet with an undercurrent of mild acidity and modest fiber content. Hold with both hands.
items.intent: Present an unmodified piece of fruit as a complete, composed dessert course.

items.prompt: Give me tasting notes for waiting for a very important email.
items.response: Color: Inbox white, constantly refreshing. Nose: Hope with oxidizing edges. Palate: Nervous sweetness up front, a long savory mid-palate of dread, then the inbox notification that is — not from them. Finish: F5. F5. F5.
items.intent: Dramatize digital anticipation and the particular ache of waiting for news.

items.prompt: Write play-by-play commentary for someone trying to untangle headphone cables
items.response: The tangle is severe — three loops minimum, one knot of uncertain origin. Bishop approaches with both hands. She identifies a loose end — pulls gently — the knot TIGHTENS, as they do! She reverses course, feeding slack back in! She isolates the inner loop! Threading through now — left hand holds the tension, right hand pilots the jack through — it's COMING FREE! One final pull and the cable hangs straight! She holds it up under the light. No remaining loops. Completely, impossibly straight. We may never see this again.
items.intent: Celebrate patience and spatial problem-solving in the face of a small infuriating object.

items.prompt: Write an ode to the office supply closet that's always locked
items.response:
O cedar door of promised pens,
you yield to none without a key—
home to rubber bands and lens
of glasses lost in 2003.
items.intent: Lament the inaccessible supply closet as a symbol of bureaucratic gatekeeping.

items.prompt: Write a ballad about typing your email address wrong in the password reset field
items.response:
I summoned the link to the email I'd typed,
And waited in darkness while hope was still ripe.
No email arrived. No reset. No grace.
I checked every folder. The link left no trace.

Then slowly I saw it — gmal, not gmail —
A single dropped letter, a broken-winged sail.
The inbox of gmal is a place none may know.
My password reset is performing its show
For nobody there, in the dark, down below.
items.intent: Dramatize the cascade of failed recovery caused by a single-character typo.

items.prompt: tanka about a website redesign that hid all the features I used
items.response:
the settings: relocated
the button I pressed each day
now a hidden sub-sub-menu
fresh, they called it; new; improved—
I learned your face; you changed it
items.intent: grieving the loss of familiar interface patterns after a forced redesign

items.prompt: Write a dry acrostic poem using the corporate term SILO
items.response:
Separate and safe from other teams.
In here, we accomplish all our dreams.
Less collaboration, more our lane.
Only connect at the campaign.
items.intent: Capture the isolationist logic of siloed teams in large organizations.

items.prompt: Write a clerihew for the marketing director who describes everything as "disruptive."
items.response:
Lavinia Stoke
disrupted the people she spoke.
The memo was disruptive, the font was bold,
and the water-cooler chat disrupted the cold.
items.intent: Lampoon the marketing executive whose overuse of buzzwords hollows out all meaning.

items.prompt: Give me a toast for someone who's leaving after a merger made their position redundant — they took it with grace
items.response: To someone made redundant by a spreadsheet that never once sat through a difficult quarter with us. The org chart has been reorganized. The debt we owe you has not. Carry our thanks into whatever comes next — there's plenty of it.
items.intent: Offer a dignified, warm farewell to a colleague who handled corporate elimination of their role with admirable composure.

items.prompt: Draft a press release from a startup that claims to have disrupted the concept of Monday.
items.response:
FOR IMMEDIATE RELEASE

Chronoshft Announces Full Disruption of Monday

SAN FRANCISCO, CA — Chronoshft today announced it has successfully disrupted Monday, the traditionally underperforming first unit of the five-day industrial workweek.

Through its proprietary WeekLayer OS platform, Chronoshft reconfigures team schedules so that no work begins before 11 a.m. on the first day of the work period, a day the company now designates internally as "Proto-Tuesday."

The company reports a 34% increase in self-reported enthusiasm and a 19% reduction in what its dashboard calls "temporal dread."

"Monday was designed by industrialists. We were designed in a co-working space in the Mission," said CEO and Day Architect Finn Sato. "We think the better product wins."

Chronoshft is currently pre-revenue. Its Series A closed at $12M.

Contact: time@chronoshft.io
items.intent: Skewer the startup tendency to describe ordinary schedule flexibility as technological disruption worthy of venture capital.

items.prompt: Draft a mission statement for a company that professionally re-rolls partially used tape dispensers
items.response: We restore the resource. No more starting mid-roll. No more torn edges. Just clean, committed adhesion from the very first pull.
items.intent: Attach operational excellence language to a made-up micro-task restoration service

items.prompt: Write a wikipedia stub article about the phenomenon of grocery store music affecting shopping speed
items.response: **Retail Music Tempo Effect** is a consumer psychology phenomenon in which the tempo of background music in a retail environment measurably influences the pace of shoppers' movement and the volume of their purchases. Milliman (1982) demonstrated that slower music correlates with reduced walking speed and higher sales figures. Supermarket chains now employ acoustical consultants to optimize in-store playlists. See also: [[Muzak]], [[Consumer psychology]], [[Atmospherics]].
items.intent: Explain why supermarkets play slow music and how it manipulates you into spending more.

items.prompt: Give me a tarot reading for a real estate agent in a down market.
items.response: The Four of Pentacles grips the listing like a drowning commission. The Hanged Man: everything is suspended and you cannot speed up rates. The Hermit walks empty open houses and considers. The Ace of Wands says something adjacent to real estate — staging, consulting, rental management — is the bridge. The World reversed: the market is not done contracting. The Chariot: move before it tells you to.
items.intent: Prompt a commission-dependent professional to diversify before the market forces it.

items.prompt: Write a Magic 8-Ball response to whether quantum computing will break current encryption before 2035
items.response: Outlook not so good — for your encrypted messages, that is. For everyone else's, same.
items.intent: Forecast the cryptographic vulnerability of current security infrastructure to quantum decryption.
